title: SEGMENT 16 — Retroviruses as tools: gene therapy vectors

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Retroviruses as tools: gene therapy vectors
Now let's flip the frame again.

If a retrovirus can stably insert a sequence of DNA into a host chromosome, and if we can engineer the contents of that virus, we have the world's most efficient way of installing a new gene into a cell.
That insight is the foundation of modern gene therapy.

The basic recipe: take a retrovirus, delete the viral protein-coding genes — gag, pol, env — but keep the cis-acting elements that the virus needs to be packaged and reverse transcribed and integrated, namely the LTRs, the PBS, the PPT, and the psi packaging signal.
In place of the viral genes, insert your therapeutic gene of interest.
Then produce the vector by providing gag, pol, and env in trans from a separate construct or producer cell line.

The vector RNA gets packaged into a particle containing all the viral machinery, but the particle can only do one round of infection — it transduces the target cell, integrates, expresses the therapeutic gene.
No further replication, because the viral genes aren't in the vector.
That's the architecture.
The history is a roller coaster.
The first vectors were gammaretroviral, derived from murine leukemia virus.

These were used in early clinical trials for adenosine deaminase deficient severe combined immunodeficiency, ADA-SCID — a fatal pediatric disease where a missing enzyme prevents normal T cell development.
The earliest trials in the 1990s were partial successes.

The trials for X-linked SCID, X-SCID, looked even more promising — children with no functional T cells received their own bone marrow stem cells transduced with a working IL-2 receptor gamma chain gene, and their immune systems were reconstituted.
But around 2002, several of those X-SCID children developed T-cell leukemia.
Four out of about twenty in the French trial.

The cause was traced to insertional mutagenesis: the gammaretroviral vector had integrated near a proto-oncogene called LMO2, and the strong viral LTR enhancer activated LMO2 transcription, contributing to T-cell transformation.
The field reeled.
The response, over the following decade, was a series of safety improvements.
Self-inactivating vectors, SIN vectors, were developed in which the U3 region of the 3' LTR is deleted.
During reverse transcription, U3 sequences are copied to both LTRs — so deleting U3 from the 3' LTR results in a provirus where both LTRs are missing their U3 enhancer-promoter regions.
The vector still expresses the therapeutic gene from an internal promoter you provide, but the LTR can no longer activate nearby cellular genes.
This dramatically reduces the insertional mutagenesis risk.
In parallel, lentiviral vectors — derived from HIV-1 — replaced gammaretroviral vectors as the workhorse for most applications.
Lentiviral vectors have two big advantages.
First, lentiviruses can transduce non-dividing cells, opening up neurons, hematopoietic stem cells, post-mitotic cell types.
Second, lentiviral integration site preferences differ from gammaretroviral ones — lentivirus prefers gene bodies, not promoters, and is somewhat less prone to activate nearby genes.

Modern lentiviral vectors are SIN, third-generation — meaning the helper functions are split across multiple plasmids to prevent recombination back to a replication-competent virus — and use a heterologous envelope, typically the VSV-G glycoprotein from vesicular stomatitis virus, which gives the vector broad tropism.
They are produced in HEK293T cells by co-transfecting four to five plasmids.
The clinical success rate has been substantial in the past decade.
Strimvelis for ADA-SCID.
Zynteglo for transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia, where a functional beta-globin gene is delivered.
Skysona for adrenoleukodystrophy.
The CAR-T cell therapies — Kymriah, Yescarta, Tecartus, Breyanzi, Abecma, Carvykti — all use lentiviral vectors to install the chimeric antigen receptor into the patient's T cells.
Those drugs have transformed the treatment of certain B-cell leukemias and lymphomas and multiple myeloma.
So a virus that, in its wild-type form, causes one of the worst infectious diseases of the modern era, has — through careful engineering — become a tool that cures previously fatal genetic diseases.
That arc, from pathogen to therapeutic vector, is one of the more remarkable stories in modern medicine.
